words.definition: worried and nervous about something that might happen
example_sentences: She felt anxious about the job interview all week.
He gets anxious in large crowds.
The wait made everyone anxious.
words.pronunciation_tip: ANGK-shus — two syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I am nervous always' — 'anxious' implies ongoing worry, not just immediate nerves.

words.definition: unable to cope with something because it is too intense
example_sentences: She felt overwhelmed with gratitude when they surprised her.
He was overwhelmed by the amount of information.
After the loss, she was completely overwhelmed with grief.
words.pronunciation_tip: oh-ver-WELMD — stress on third syllable.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I have too much emotion' — say 'I feel overwhelmed'.

words.definition: feeling happy because something bad did not happen
example_sentences: She was so relieved when she heard he was safe.
I'm relieved that the exam is finally over.
He looked visibly relieved after the announcement.
words.pronunciation_tip: rih-LEEVD — two syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I am happy that the problem is finished' — say 'I'm relieved'.

words.definition: feeling annoyed because you cannot do or achieve something
example_sentences: She was frustrated by the lack of progress.
He gets frustrated when people don't listen.
I'm frustrated that nothing seems to be changing.
words.pronunciation_tip: frus-TRAY-tid — three syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I am annoyed because I can't do it' — say 'I feel frustrated'.

words.definition: very shocked and upset because of something terrible
example_sentences: She was devastated when she heard the news.
He was absolutely devastated by the loss.
The family was devastated by the accident.
words.pronunciation_tip: DEV-uh-stay-tid — four syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'she was very sad' for extreme grief — say 'she was devastated'.

words.definition: happy and satisfied with your life or situation
example_sentences: She's perfectly content living in a small flat.
He seemed content with his decision.
You don't need a lot of money to feel content.
words.pronunciation_tip: kun-TENT — stress on second syllable.
words.common_mistake: Don't confuse content (adjective = satisfied, kun-TENT) with content (noun = material, KON-tent).

words.definition: feeling a warm longing for a happy past
example_sentences: The old song made her feel nostalgic.
He gets nostalgic whenever he smells fresh bread.
She felt nostalgic looking at old photographs.
words.pronunciation_tip: nos-TAL-jik — three syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I miss the past and feel warm about it' — say 'I feel nostalgic'.

words.definition: feeling bitter and angry because of unfair treatment
example_sentences: She felt resentful about being passed over for promotion.
He's grown resentful after years of being underappreciated.
A resentful employee is unlikely to perform well.
words.pronunciation_tip: rih-ZENT-ful — three syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I am angry because it was unfair' — say 'I feel resentful'.

words.definition: having no interest in or concern about something
example_sentences: He was completely indifferent to her opinion.
She was indifferent to the result — it didn't affect her.
His indifference to the situation made her angry.
words.pronunciation_tip: in-DIF-er-unt — four syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'he doesn't care' — say 'he is indifferent' in more formal contexts.

words.definition: slightly afraid or worried about something in the future
example_sentences: She was apprehensive about starting the new job.
He felt apprehensive before the performance.
It's natural to feel apprehensive before a big change.
words.pronunciation_tip: ap-rih-HEN-siv — four syllables, stress on third.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'afraid' for slight worry about the future — say 'apprehensive'.

words.definition: extremely happy and excited, usually because of success
example_sentences: She was elated when she got the news about her acceptance.
He was absolutely elated after the team won.
The whole city felt elated after the victory.
words.pronunciation_tip: ih-LAY-tid — three syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'very happy' for extreme joy — say 'elated'.

words.definition: a feeling of deep sadness without an obvious reason
example_sentences: There was a melancholy to the autumn evening.
He fell into a melancholy mood after the holiday ended.
Her music has a beautiful, melancholy quality.
words.pronunciation_tip: MEL-un-kol-ee — four syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'a sad feeling about nothing' — say 'a sense of melancholy'.

words.definition: made to feel ashamed or stupid in front of others
example_sentences: She felt completely humiliated by his comment.
He was humiliated in front of the whole team.
Public humiliation can have lasting effects.
words.pronunciation_tip: hyoo-MIL-ee-ay-tid — five syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'she was embarrassed badly' — say 'she felt humiliated'.

words.definition: having two opposite feelings about something at the same time
example_sentences: She felt conflicted about leaving — excited but also sad.
He's conflicted about whether to accept the offer.
I feel conflicted — I want to go but also want to stay.
words.pronunciation_tip: kun-FLIK-tid — three syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I have two feelings' — say 'I feel conflicted'.

words.definition: feeling thankful for something someone did for you
example_sentences: She was deeply grateful for all the support she received.
He felt grateful to have such loyal friends.
I'm so grateful you were there when I needed you.
words.pronunciation_tip: GRAYT-ful — two syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I say thank you' as an adjective — say 'I feel grateful'.

words.definition: easily annoyed, especially when tired or unwell
example_sentences: She's been irritable all day — she didn't sleep well.
Don't talk to him when he's irritable.
Being hungry makes me very irritable.
words.pronunciation_tip: IR-ih-tuh-bul — four syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'she is always angry' — 'irritable' means easily annoyed, often temporarily.

words.definition: showing kindness and concern for people who are suffering
example_sentences: She is one of the most compassionate people I know.
A good doctor must be compassionate as well as skilled.
He responded to the tragedy in a compassionate way.
words.pronunciation_tip: kum-PASH-un-ut — four syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'she is kind to sad people' — say 'she is compassionate'.

words.definition: lacking confidence in yourself or your position
example_sentences: He's very insecure about his abilities.
She felt insecure in a room full of more experienced people.
Insecurity can make people behave defensively.
words.pronunciation_tip: in-sih-KYOOR — three syllables, stress on third.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'he doesn't have confidence' as an adjective — say 'he feels insecure'.

words.definition: feeling extremely happy and excited, almost beyond reason
example_sentences: She felt euphoric when she crossed the finish line.
The crowd was euphoric after the final goal.
He described a euphoric feeling after the race.
words.pronunciation_tip: yoo-FOR-ik — three syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'extremely happy' for intense joy — say 'euphoric'.

words.definition: feeling discouraged because something has not gone as hoped
example_sentences: She was disheartened by the lack of progress.
Don't get disheartened — keep trying.
He felt disheartened after yet another rejection.
words.pronunciation_tip: dis-HAR-tend — three syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'she lost hope' — say 'she felt disheartened'.
